item.title: CS336 Lecture 1: Tokenization
item.description:
This post covers Tokenization from Stanford CS336: Language Modeling from Scratch, Lecture 1.

Contents

  1. Tokenization

Tokenization

  • Tokenizer: A class that performs encode and decode operations.
  • Vocabulary size: The number of distinct token types.

Tokenization Examples

You can try it yourself at tiktokenizer.

  • Spaces are part of tokens. They usually appear at the beginning of a token.
  • hello and hello are different tokens.
  • Numbers are grouped from left to right during tokenization.

Compression Ratio

  • $\text{compression ratio} = \text{num\_bytes} / \text{num\_tokens}$
  • Examples (GPT-4o):
    • Hello World
      • Hello(5) + space(1) + World(5) = 11 bytes
      • Tokens: 2
      • 5.5 bytes per token
    • 안녕 세상 (Korean)
      • 안(3) + 녕(3) + space(1) + 세(3) + 상(3) = 13 bytes
      • Tokens: 4
      • 3.25 bytes per token

Character-based Tokenization

  • Many characters rarely appear, making it highly inefficient.
  • Low compression ratio.

Byte-based Tokenization

  • 1 byte = 8 bits → $2^8 = 256$ possible values.
  • Vocab size: 256
  • Compression ratio: 1
  • Sequence length becomes very long. Since Attention is $O(n^2)$, computation cost increases significantly.

Word-based Tokenization

  • The number of words is extremely large, and many are rare.
  • Fixed vocab size cannot be determined.
  • e.g., ChatGPTized → <UNK>

Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)

  • Learns vocabulary by repeatedly merging frequently occurring sequences in text.
  • Starts with each byte as an initial token, then merges frequently occurring byte pairs across the entire text.
  • Frequent multi-character sequences become a single token.
  • Rare sequences are split into multiple tokens.
  • e.g., ChatGPTized → Chat, GPT, ized
  • e.g., ChatGPTized → Chat, G, PT, ized

item.title: CLIP
item.description:
In this post, we will take a look at “Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision,” published by OpenAI in 2021.
https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/radford21a/radford21a.pdf

Table of Contents

  1. Inference Examples
  2. Training Method
  3. Model
  4. Additional Training Techniques
  5. Results

Inference Examples

  • Zero-shot inference results

    Source: Radford, Alec, et al. “Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2021, Figure 11.

Training Method

  • Training

    • Given a batch of N (image, text) pairs, representations of both the images and texts are generated.
    • Instead of using labels, text is used, so there is no need for dataset labeling during dataset creation. Source: Radford, Alec, et al. “Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2021, Figure 1, (1).
      • An image encoder and a text encoder are trained.
      • The model is trained to maximize the cosine similarity between image embedding and text embedding of the actual N pairs.
      • For each embedding, the output from the [CLS] token of the image encoder is used for the image, and the [EOS] token output from the text encoder is used for text.
      • Contrastive objective: The model is also trained to minimize the cosine similarity between $N^2 - N$ negative image-text pairs.
      • The final symmetric cross entropy loss is obtained by performing both: (1) classifying text embeddings based on image embeddings, and (2) classifying image embeddings based on text embeddings, then calculating cross entropy for both.
    • The larger the mini-batch size, the more detailed the representations become.

  • Inference

    • Obtain the image feature embedding.
    • Obtain the embeddings for all possible texts.
    • Compute the cosine similarity between image and text embeddings.
      Source: Radford, Alec, et al. “Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2021, Figure 1, (2), (3).
      • Image encoder: vision backbone, generates the feature representation of an image.
      • Text encoder: acts as a linear classifier.
      • Once text encoders are computed, the zero-shot classifier can be cached to reduce inference cost.
    • Inference can be performed without any task-specific training.

Model

  • Image encoder

    • Uses ResNet-50 or Vision Transformer.
    • Output size: 512

  • Text encoder

    • 64M parameters, 12 layers, 512-wide, 8 attention heads
    • Output size: 512
    • Vocabulary size: 49,152
    • Max sequence length: 76

  • Dataset: WebImageText (WIT)

    • 400M image-text pairs collected from the internet.
    • Text must include at least one of 500,000 queries (words that appeared at least 100 times in English Wikipedia).
    • Up to 20,000 (image, text) pairs per query.
    • The word count is similar to that of WebText used in GPT-2 training.

  • Loss: InfoNCE Loss

    • $ {\mathcal{L}}\_{\text{CLIP}}= \frac{1}{2} [ \underbrace{-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \frac{\exp(sim(I_i,T_i)/\tau)} {\sum_{j=1}^{N}\exp(sim(I_i,T_j) / \tau)}}_{\text{image-to-text loss}} + \underbrace{- \frac{1}{N} \sum^{N}\_{i=1} \log \frac{\exp(sim(T_i, I_i)/\tau)}{\sum^{N}\_{j=1} \exp(sim(T_j, I_i) / \tau)}}\_\text{{text-to-image loss}} ] $

  • Parameters

    • Image and text encoders are trained from scratch, without any pre-trained weights.
    • Variants: ResNet-50, ResNet-101, ResNet-50x4, ResNet50x16, ResNet50x64
    • Variants: ViT-B/32, ViT-B/16, ViT-L/14
    • 32 epochs
    • Initial hyperparameters: performed grid search, random search, and manual tuning using 1 epoch of training on the smallest model
    • Mini-batch size: 32,768
      • Since training is performed on alt-text collected from the web, there may be similar images or alt-texts, so large batch training is necessary.

  • Training time

    • CLIP with ResNet50x64 backbone: trained for 18 days on 592 V100 GPUs.
    • CLIP with ViT-L/14 backbone: trained for 12 days on 256 V100 GPUs.

Additional Training Techniques

  • Prompt Engineering
    • In the absence of prompts, homonyms such as “crane” (the bird or the machine) or “major” (department or meaning rank) caused problems.
    • Using prompts like “A photo of a {label}” increased ImageNet accuracy by 1.3%.
    • For datasets with specific categories:
      • Oxford-IIIT: “A photo of a {label}, a type of pet.”
      • Food101: “A photo of a {label}, a type of food.”
      • FGVC Aircraft: “A photo of a {label}, a type of aircraft.”
      • Satellite image classification datasets: “A satellite photo of a {label}.”
  • Ensembling
    • Ensembled several zero-shot classifiers.
      • Examples: “A photo of a big {label}”, “A photo of a small {label}”
      • Ensembled in the embedding space. Used cache to reduce cost.
      • For ImageNet: Used 80 different context prompts and ensembled their predictions, achieving a 3.5% accuracy improvement.

Results

  • Zero-shot performance

    • Compared with a ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet and then fine-tuned via linear probing.

      • On many datasets, zero-shot CLIP outperformed.

    • Zero-shot CLIP vs Few-shot linear probe CLIP (average score across 20 datasets).

      • 4-shot CLIP showed performance comparable to zero-shot CLIP.

  • Linear probe CLIP vs state-of-the-art models.

  • Natural Distribution Shift

    • Zero-shot CLIP achieved performance comparable to models trained specifically on ImageNet.

item.title: LLaMA
item.description:
In this post, we will take a look at LLaMA, introduced by Meta in 2023.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13971

Table of Contents

  1. Background
  2. Pre-Training Data
  3. Model
  4. Results

Background

  • The 2020 Scaling Law paper argued that the performance of language models is mainly determined by the number of parameters, the size of the dataset, and the amount of compute used for training.
  • Afterwards, ultra-large models such as Google’s PaLM, which scale the number of parameters up to 540B, spread the belief that “bigger is better.”
  • However, DeepMind’s Chinchilla study found that, given the same compute budget, training a smaller model on more data can yield similar or even better results than training a very large model on less data.
  • LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI)
    • To reduce inference costs, LLaMA favors smaller models that are trained longer on more data.
    • With parameter sizes ranging from 7B to 65B, LLaMA achieves performance comparable to the largest models.
    • LLaMA is also distinct from existing large language models in that it is trained exclusively on public data and the model weights are openly released.

Pre-Training Data

  • Dataset

    • English CommonCrawl
      • Deduplication applied at the line level.
      • Non-English documents removed.
      • Low-quality data filtered out using an n-gram language model.
      • Used a linear model to classify pages referenced in Wikipedia, and removed those not referenced.
    • C4
      • Deduplication at the line level.
      • Non-English documents removed.
      • Quality filtered by punctuation marks, number of words, and number of sentences.
    • Github
      • Only Apache, BSD, and MIT license repositories included.
      • Quality filtered by line count and ratio of English/numeric content.
      • Removed boilerplate.
      • Deduplication at the file level by exact match.
    • Wikipedia
      • Included 20 languages.
      • Hyperlinks, comments, and boilerplate removed.
    • Gutenberg and Books3
      • Deduplication at the book level when overlap exceeds 90%.
    • Arxiv
      • Used LaTeX files.
      • Removed content before the first section.
    • Stack Exchange
      • HTML tags removed.
      • Answers ordered by score. Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 1.

  • Tokenizer

    • Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)
    • SentencePiece
    • Used a total of 1.4T tokens for training. (For reference: PaLM-540B was trained on 780B tokens, Chinchilla-70B on 1.4T tokens.)

Model

  • Architecture
    • Used RMSNorm to normalize input instead of the output.
    • SwiGLU activation function.
    • Adopted Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE), a type of relative positional embedding.
  • Optimizer
    • AdamW.
    • Cosine learning rate schedule.
    • Weight decay.
    • Gradient clipping.
    • 2,000 warmup steps. Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 2.
  • Efficient Implementation
    • Causal multi-head attention: operations were not computed for masked positions.
    • Checkpointing: expensive activations were stored for reuse, implemented manually rather than using PyTorch autograd.
    • Used 2,048 A100 GPUs (80GB each), processing 380 tokens per GPU per second; total training took 21 days.

Results

  • Despite being relatively small in size, LLaMA achieves performance comparable to large language models.
  • Common Sense Reasoning Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 3.
  • Closed-book Question Answering
    Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 4.
    Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 5.
  • Reading Comprehension

    Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 6.
  • Mathematical Reasoning
    • Task involving solving middle and high school math problems written in LaTeX.
    • Minerva: PaLM model fine-tuned on Arxiv and Math Web Pages.
      Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 7.
  • Code Generation

    Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 8.
  • Massive Multitask Language Understanding
    • Unlike Chinchilla, Gopher, and PaLM (trained on up to 2TB of book data), LLaMA was trained on just 177GB of data but demonstrated similar performance.
      Source: Touvron, Hugo, et al. “Llama: Open and efficient foundation language models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.13971 (2023), Table 9.

item.title: Donut vs. Pix2Struct Comparison
item.description:
In this post, we’ll review and compare two end-to-end deep learning models capable of document understanding from images, without relying on OCR:

  • Donut: OCR-free Document Understanding Transformer (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.15664)
  • Pix2Struct: Screenshot Parsing as Pretraining for Visual Language Understanding (https://proceedings.mlr.press/v202/lee23g/lee23g.pdf)

Table of Contents

  1. Model Architecture
  2. Pre-training
  3. Fine-tuning

Model Architecture

Both models use an image encoder and a text decoder.

Encoder

  • Donut

    • Utilizes the Swin Transformer.
      Source: Liu, Ze, et al. “Swin transformer: Hierarchical vision transformer using shifted windows.” Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF international conference on computer vision. 2021, Figure 1

      • Each layer uses patches of various sizes (Hierarchical Feature Map).
      • Applies Shifted Window Multi Self-Attention for local window operations within image regions.

    • Experiments with various backbones show Swin Transformer backbone yields the best performance for Donut.
      Source: Kim, Geewook, et al. “Ocr-free document understanding transformer.” European Conference on Computer Vision. Cham: Springer Nature Switzerland, 2022, Figure 7

  • Pix2Struct

    • Uses a ViT (Vision Transformer) capable of handling variable-resolution inputs.
      Source: Lee, Kenton, et al. “Pix2struct: Screenshot parsing as pretraining for visual language understanding.” International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2023, Figure 2

      • Images are resized (keeping aspect ratio) to maximize the number of extracted patches.
      • Utilizes 2D absolute positional embeddings.
      • Performs well even with extreme aspect ratios.

Decoder

  • Donut
    • Uses the first four layers of BART as the decoder.
    • Weights are initialized from a pre-trained multi-lingual BART.
  • Pix2Struct
    • Utilizes a ViT-based decoder.

Pre-training

  • Donut

    • Tested four pre-training strategies: No pre-training, Classification, Captioning, and Reading. The “reading” approach, aimed at language understanding, was chosen.
    • Performs a pseudo-OCR task: predicting the next token given the image and previous context.
    • Public data: Used IIT-CDIP (11M scanned document images) with pseudo-text labels generated by CLOVA OCR API.
    • Synthetic data: Used the Synthetic Document Generator (SynthDoG) and Wikipedia to create 0.5M samples each in Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and English.
      • Backgrounds are sampled from ImageNet
      • Words/expressions are sampled from Wikipedia.
      • Example synthetic data below:
        Source: Kim, Geewook, et al. “Ocr-free document understanding transformer.” European Conference on Computer Vision. Cham: Springer Nature Switzerland, 2022, Figure 4

  • Pix2Struct

    • Aims to understand image structure through reading, mask restoration, and alt-text generation.

    • Data:

      • Uses webpage screenshots and associated HTML.
      • Only uses nodes with visible elements.
      • Uses only text, filename, and alt-text (ignores tag, style, title, URL).
      • Viewport size: (1024, 1024).
      • Masks 50% of the text.

    • Pre-training tasks example: Source: Lee, Kenton, et al. “Pix2struct: Screenshot parsing as pretraining for visual language understanding.” International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2023, Figure 3

      • <C++>: Generates the unmasked portion of HTML. Similar to OCR.
      • <Python>: Generates the masked portion of HTML. Similar to masked language modeling.
      • img_alt=C++: Generates alt-text from the image. Similar to image captioning.

    • Curriculum Learning:

      • Adds a warmup stage with a focus on reading tasks.
      • Uses BooksCorpus data.
      • Leads to more stable and faster convergence.
      • Improves fine-tuning performance.
      • Example data for the reading task: Source: Lee, Kenton, et al. “Pix2struct: Screenshot parsing as pretraining for visual language understanding.” International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2023, Figure 6

Training / Fine-tuning

  • Donut

    • Defines all downstream tasks as JSON prediction problems. Source: Kim, Geewook, et al. “Ocr-free document understanding transformer.” European Conference on Computer Vision. Cham: Springer Nature Switzerland, 2022, Figure 3

    • For example, a document classification task predicts the sequence [START_class] [memo] [END_class], which is then parsed into {"class":"memo"}.

    • Document Classification

      • RVL-CDIP: Dataset with 16 classes such as letter, memo, email, etc.

    • Document Information Extraction

      • CORD: English receipt dataset with 30 fields (menu name, quantity, total amount, etc.).
      • Ticket: Chinese train ticket dataset with 8 fields (ticket number, departure station, etc.).
      • Business Card: Japanese business card dataset with 11 fields (name, company, etc.).
      • Receipt: Korean receipt dataset with 81 fields (store info, payment info, etc.).

    • Document Visual Question Answering

      • DocVQA: Document image QA dataset with questions and answers paired to document images.
      • Questions are used as prompts, e.g., <vqa><question>what is the price of choco mochi?</question></vqa>

    • Results

      • RVL-CDIP: Source: Kim, Geewook, et al. “Ocr-free document understanding transformer.” European Conference on Computer Vision. Cham: Springer Nature Switzerland, 2022, Table 1

      • Information Extraction: Source: Kim, Geewook, et al. “Ocr-free document understanding transformer.” European Conference on Computer Vision. Cham: Springer Nature Switzerland, 2022, Table 2

  • Pix2Struct
    • For VQA tasks, the question is attached at the top of the input image.
    • The Donut model is finetuned and used as the backbone.
    • VQA Tasks
      • ChartQA: Dataset consisting of questions and answers about charts.
      • AI2D: Multiple-choice QA dataset for scientific images.
      • OCR-VQA: Questions about the title, author, genre, etc., for book cover images.
      • RefExp: App screenshots with widget bounding boxes and descriptive sentences; the label is “true” if the box matches the description, and “false” otherwise.
      • DocVQA: Dataset with document images and corresponding QA pairs. Pix2Struct outperforms the previous SOTA Donut. However, it underperforms compared to UDOP, which relies on OCR, pre-trained text/image encoders, and IIT-CDIP in-domain pretraining. Still, Pix2Struct achieves solid performance without any in-domain pretraining.
      • InfographicVQA: Dataset with questions and answers about infographics.
    • Captioning Tasks
      • Widget Captioning: App screenshots with widget bounding boxes; each caption describes the widget’s function.
      • Screen2Words: Captions describing the main functionality of a given screenshot page.
      • TextCaps: Dataset of images paired with captions.
    • Results Source: Lee, Kenton, et al. “Pix2struct: Screenshot parsing as pretraining for visual language understanding.” International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2023, Table 1

item.title: LayoutLMv2 Review
item.description:
In this post, we will review Microsoft’s 2021 paper: “LayoutLMv2: Multi-modal Pre-training for Visually-rich Document Understanding.”
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.14740.pdf

Table of Contents

  1. Model Architecture
  2. Pre-training Tasks
  3. Training

Model Architecture

Text Embedding

  • Uses text sequences extracted via OCR.
  • Utilizes the WordPiece tokenizer.
  • Prepends [CLS] to the sequence and [SEP] to the end of each text segment.
  • Pads with [PAD] tokens until the maximum sequence length $L$ is reached.
  • Applies 1D positional embeddings.
  • Assigns each token a segment $ s_i \in \{[A], [B]\} $.
  • The final $i$-th $(0 \leq i < L)$ text embedding is:
    • $ t_i = TokEmb(w_i) + PosEmb1D(i) + SegEmb(s_i) $

Visual Embedding

  • Resizes the input image to 224×224 pixels.
  • Uses ResNeXt-FPN as the visual backbone to extract feature maps; its parameters are updated during backpropagation.
  • The feature map is average-pooled to width $W$ and height $H$ (paper uses $W = H = 7$).
  • Flattens this to a $W \times H$-length vector $ VisTokEmb(I) $.
  • Applies a linear projection layer to match the size of the text embeddings.
  • Since CNNs do not inherently encode position, the same 1D positional embeddings as text tokens are added.
  • The segment used for visual tokens is [C].
  • The $i$-th $(0 \leq i < WH)$ visual embedding is:
    • $ v_i = Proj(VisTokEmb(I)_i) + PosEmb1D(i) + SegEmb([C]) $

Layout Embedding

  • Embeds the bounding box information that comes from OCR results.
  • Normalizes the box coordinates to integers in [0, 1000].
  • For the $i$-th $(0 \leq i < WH + L)$ text/visual token with $box_i = (x_{min}, x_{max}, y_{min}, y_{max}, width, height)$, the layout embedding is:
    • $ l_i = Concat(PosEmb2D_x(x_{min}, x_{max}, width), PosEmb2D_y(y_{min}, y_{max}, height)) $
  • For visual tokens, the bounding box corresponds to the position in the $W \times H$ grid. For $W = H = 7$, $box_0 = (0, 0, 142, 142, 142, 142)$ where $142 = int(1000/7)$.
  • Special tokens [CLS], [SEP], [PAD] use $ box_{PAD} = (0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0) $.

Multi-modal Encoder

  • Input
    • Concatenates the visual embeddings and text embeddings into a single sequence:
      • $ X = \{\textbf{v}_0, ..., \textbf{v}_{WH-1}, \textbf{t}_0, ..., \textbf{t}_{L-1}\} $
    • Adds the corresponding layout embedding to the above sequence for the final input:
      • For $0 \leq i < WH + L$, $ \textbf{x}_i^{(0)} = X_i + \textbf{l}_i $
  • Spatial-Aware Self-Attention
    • Let hidden size be $d_{head}$ and projections $\textbf{W}^Q, \textbf{W}^K, \textbf{W}^V$. The self-attention score is:
      • $ \alpha_{ij} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_{head}}} (\textbf{x}_i \textbf{W}^Q) (\textbf{x}_j \textbf{W}^K)^\top $
    • $ \textbf{b}^{(1D)} $ encodes relative distance in the token sequence.
    • $ \textbf{b}^{(2D_x)}, \textbf{b}^{(2D_y)} $ encode the spatial distance between token bounding boxes in x and y directions.
    • The spatial-aware attention score is:
      • $ \alpha_{ij}^{\prime} = \alpha_{ij} + \textbf{b}^{(1D)} + \textbf{b}^{(2D_x)} + \textbf{b}^{(2D_y)} $
    • The output vector $h_i$ is:
      • $ \textbf{h}_i = \displaystyle{\sum_{j}} \frac{\exp(\alpha_{ij}^{\prime})}{\sum_{k} \exp(\alpha_{ik}^{\prime})} \textbf{x}_j \textbf{W}^V $


Source: Xu, Yang, et al. “Layoutlmv2: Multi-modal pre-training for visually-rich document understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2012.14740 (2020), Figure 1.

Pre-training Tasks

Masked Visual-Language Modeling (MVLM)

  • Teaches the model to better understand language using cross-modality information.
  • Randomly masks text tokens and requires the model to predict them.
  • In this paper, 15% of text tokens are masked; 80% are replaced with [MASK], 10% with a random token from the vocabulary, and 10% remain unchanged.
  • The corresponding regions in the image are masked as well.
  • Layout information is left unmasked.
  • The final cross-entropy loss is computed by feeding the last output of the masked token to a classifier.

Text-Image Alignment (TIA)

  • Randomly selects a token line and covers its region in the image. Lines are covered at the line level rather than the word level.
  • 15% of lines are covered in the paper.
  • The model predicts whether the output of a token corresponds to [Covered] or [Not Covered] using a classifier attached to the output of the covered tokens.
  • Tokens masked for MVLM are excluded from TIA loss calculation.

Text-Image Matching (TIM)

  • Classifier attached to the [CLS] output to predict whether the image and text come from the same document.
  • The original image is used as a positive sample.
  • Negative samples are produced by swapping with images from other documents or omitting the image (missing image).
  • 15% of images are swapped and 5% are omitted in experiments.
  • Negative samples are also masked and covered; in negative TIM, all TIA targets are [Covered].
  • Binary cross-entropy loss is used.

Training

Data


Source: Xu, Yang, et al. “Layoutlmv2: Multi-modal pre-training for visually-rich document understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2012.14740 (2020), Table 1.

Settings

  • The text encoder uses UniLMv2.
  • The visual embedding uses ResNeXt-FPN, the backbone from Mask-RCNN.
  • Other parameters are initialized randomly.
  • All parameters are trained end-to-end.

Pre-training

  • Utilizes samples from the IIT-CDIP dataset.
  • Sets maximum sequence length $L=512$, all text tokens are assigned segment [A].
  • The vision encoder’s output shape is set to $W=H=7$, producing a feature map of 49 tokens.

Fine-tuning

  • For document-level classification, the [CLS] token’s output is used.
  • Entity extraction and DocVQA tasks add a task-specific head to the model.

item.title: UniLMv2 Review
item.description:
In this post, we review Microsoft’s 2020 paper “UniLMv2: Pseudo-Masked Language Models for Unified Language Model Pre-Training”.
http://proceedings.mlr.press/v119/bao20a/bao20a.pdf

Table of Contents

  1. Unified Language Model
  2. Pre-Train
  3. Fine-tuning
  4. Result

Unified Language Model

  • Before this work, language model studies were largely split between bidirectional models good at Natural Language Understanding (NLU), and autoregressive models good at Natural Language Generation (NLG).

  • This paper proposes a Unified Language Model called Pseudo-Masked Language Models (PMLM), which can perform both AutoEncoding (AE) and Partially AutoRegressive (PAR) language modeling at the same time using pseudo-masks on masked tokens—enabling both strong understanding and generation.

  • AE enables bi-directional LM training and learns the inter-relation between masked tokens and the context.

  • PAR enables sequence-to-sequence LM training and learns the intra-relation within masked spans.

  • The unified model shares parameters for both bi-directional (AE) and sequence-to-sequence (PAR) pre-training.

  • Parameters are shared as shown in the figure below for the two language model tasks:
    Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Figure 1.

  • AE uses masked tokens [MASK] or [M].

  • PAR uses additional masks [Pseudo] or [P] or sometimes the original token, depending on the factorization step.

  • The model can be trained with both modes in a single forward pass.

Pre-Train

  • Input Representation: [SOS] s1 [EOS] s2 [EOS]
  • The paper divides Masked Language Modeling (MLM) into AE, AutoRegressive (AR), and PAR models, which are differentiated by how the probabilities of masked tokens are factorized. PMLM uses both AE and PAR.
    Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Table 1.

AE Modeling

  • Some tokens in the sentence are masked ([MASK]), and the model tries to predict these.
  • For input $ x=x_1 ... x_{|x|} $, mask positions $ M={m_1, ..., m_{|M|}} $, the probability of the masked tokens is $ \prod\limits_{m∈M} p(x_m|x_{\backslash M})$.
    • $x_M={\lbrace x_m \rbrace}_{m∈M}$: all masked tokens.
    • $x_{\backslash M}$: all unmasked input tokens. $\backslash$ indicates set difference.
  • AE pre-training loss:
    • $ {\cal L_{\rm AE}}= - \displaystyle\sum_{x∈{\cal D}} \log \prod\limits_{m∈M} p(x_m|x_{\backslash M}) $
      • $ {\cal D} $: training corpus.
      • Uses cross-entropy loss.

PAR Modeling

  • Multiple tokens are masked and predicted sequentially.
  • $ p(x_M | x_{\backslash M}) = \displaystyle\prod\limits_{i=1}^{|M|} p(x_{M_i}|x_{\backslash M_{\ge i}}) \\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ = \displaystyle\prod\limits_{i=1}^{|M|} \displaystyle\prod\limits_{m∈M_i} p(x_m|x_{\backslash M_{\ge i}})$
    • At each factorization step, the model predicts one or more tokens.
    • $ M= \langle M_1, ..., M_{|M|} \rangle $: the sequence in which factors (sets of masks) are revealed.
    • $ M_i= \lbrace m_1^i, ..., m_{|M_i|}^i \rbrace $: set of positions masked at the $i$-th step.
    • $x_{M_i}={\lbrace x_m \rbrace}_{m∈M_i}$: masked tokens at the $i$-th step.
  • PAR pre-training loss:
    • $ {\cal L_{\rm AE}}= - \displaystyle\sum_{x∈{\cal D}} \Bbb E_M \log p(x_M|x_{\backslash M}) $
      • $M$ is randomly chosen for each sequence.
  • Blockwise Masking and Factorization:
    • 15% of tokens are selected. For 40% of these, an n-gram block is masked; for 60%, a single token is masked.
    • Factorization steps can be spans, making the approach partially autoregressive.
      Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Algorithm 1.
      Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Figure 2.

Pseudo-Masked LM

  • The AE (AutoEncoding) method uses [MASK] at prediction positions and treats all other tokens as context to predict all at once.
  • In AR and PAR, the model predicts tokens one (or several) at a time in sequence, and the mask positions change according to the factorization order each time.
  • As the image below shows, AE uses a single mask, but AR and PAR use different masks at each factorization step. To reflect these and facilitate learning, pseudo-masks are used.
    Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Figure 3.
  • The following image illustrates the PAR prediction process with pseudo-masks.
    Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Figure 4.
    • A pseudo-mask [P] is appended to the input sequence, while keeping the original tokens.
    • Position embeddings are the same as for the original tokens, so the location in the input sequence does not matter.
    • MLM prediction is performed by applying a softmax classifier to the final hidden state of [P].
    • The example factorization order is 4,5 → 2.
    • At t=1, the pseudo mask attends to $ x_1, x_3, x_6 $ and $ x_4, x_5 $.
    • At t=2, the pseudo mask attends to $ x_1, x_3, x_4, x_5, x_6 $ and $ x_2 $.
    • The self-attention mask for the above factorization is shown below.
      Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Figure 5.

Fine-tuning

  • Natural Language Understanding (NLU) - Classification
    • [SOS] TEXT [EOS]
    • Apply a softmax classifier to [SOS] to determine the class with the highest likelihood.
  • Natural Language Generation (NLG)
    • [SOS] SRC [EOS] TGT [EOS]
    • Source sequence: bidirectional (tokens can attend to each other).
    • Target sequence: autoregressive. Add [P] to the target tokens.
    • Select the token that maximizes the likelihood in the target sequence.

Result


Source: Bao, Hangbo, et al. “Unilmv2: Pseudo-masked language models for unified language model pre-training.” International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020, Table 2.

item.title: Label Assignment
item.description:
When training an Object Detection model, you first match the predicted candidate boxes with the Ground Truth (GT) boxes, and then compute the loss between each matched pair. This matching process is known as Label Assignment. Since improper matching can lead to incorrect learning, label assignment is a crucial step in the training pipeline. In this post, we will explore different label assignment techniques.

Table of Contents

  1. Fixed Label Assignment
  2. Dynamic Label Assignment

Fixed Label Assignment

  • Fixed Label Assignment allocates labels based on predefined thresholds.
  • IoU-based matching: If the Intersection over Union (IoU) between a predicted box and a GT box exceeds a certain threshold, the prediction is assigned to the GT.
  • Center-based matching: If the distance between the centers of the predicted box and GT box is within a threshold, the prediction is assigned to the GT.
  • Because fixed thresholds are used, there can be incorrect assignments in cases with overlapping GTs, potentially introducing confusion during training.


Source: Ge, Zheng, et al. “Ota: Optimal transport assignment for object detection.” Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2021, Figure 1.

Dynamic Label Assignment

  • Bipartite Matching

    • Finds a globally optimal matching between predicted boxes and GTs so that each predicted box is assigned to at most one GT.
    • The image below illustrates bipartite matching in DETR, where each object query passes through the decoder and prediction heads. The Hungarian algorithm is then used to match queries to GTs or the “no object” class.
      Source: Carion, Nicolas, et al. “End-to-end object detection with transformers.” European conference on computer vision. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, Figure 2.
    • Since each prediction is independently assigned to a GT, ambiguous cases are reduced and duplicate assignments are avoided.
    • However, the commonly used Hungarian algorithm has a high time complexity of O(n³), making it slow when there are many predictions or GTs.

  • OTA (Optimal Transport Assignment)

    • Defines the matching as an Optimal Transport (OT) problem, considering the matching cost between predicted boxes and GT boxes.
    • The cost is defined using cross-entropy loss for class prediction and IoU loss for location.
    • Once the cost matrix is constructed, the OT problem can be solved using the Sinkhorn-Knopp Iteration.
    • OTA algorithm illustration:

      Source: Ge, Zheng, et al. “Ota: Optimal transport assignment for object detection.” Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2021, Algorithm 1.
    • Unlike bipartite matching, OTA allows one-to-many assignments.
    • One drawback is that solving the Sinkhorn algorithm can be time-consuming.

  • simOTA

    • Solving the OT problem with the Sinkhorn algorithm increases training time by approximately 25%.
    • simOTA simplifies the process by replacing the Sinkhorn algorithm with a top-K selection method.
    • First introduced in YOLOX.
    • The cost between GT $ g_i $ and prediction $ p_j $ is calculated as follows:
      • $ c_{ij} = L_{ij}^{cls} + \lambda L_{ij}^{reg} $
    • For each GT $ g_i $, predictions with the lowest top-K costs are assigned as positives.

item.title: XLNet Review
item.description:
In this post, we’ll look at “XLNet: Generalized Autoregressive Pretraining for Language Understanding”, published by Google in 2019.
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1906.08237.pdf

Table of Contents

  1. AutoRegressive vs. AutoEncoding
  2. Permutation Language Modeling
  3. Two-Stream Self-Attention

AutoRegressive vs. AutoEncoding

AutoRegressive (AR) Language Modeling

  • Given an input token sequence, predicts the next token.
  • Pre-training maximizes the following likelihood:
    • $ \underset{θ}{\max} \log p_θ ({\bf x} ) = \displaystyle{\sum_{t=1}^{T}} \log p_θ (x_t | {\bf x_{\rm < t}}) = \displaystyle{\sum_{t=1}^{T}} \log \frac{exp(h_θ({\bf x_{\rm 1:t-1}})_t^{\top} e(x_t))}{ \sum _{x'} exp(h_θ({\bf x _{\rm 1:t-1}})^{\top} e(x'))} $
      • $ {\bf x} = [x_1, ..., x_T]$: text sequence
      • $ h_θ({\bf x _{\rm 1:t-1}}) $: context representation from a neural model like RNNs or Transformers
      • $ e(x) $: embedding of x
      • exp is used for softmax
      • Each word is predicted by referring only to the words that come before it.

AutoEncoding (AE)

  • Example: BERT
  • Randomly masks some tokens in the input sequence, and predicts the original tokens for those masked positions.
  • Pre-training maximizes the following likelihood:
    • $ \underset{θ}{\max} \log p_θ ({\bf \bar x} | {\bf \hat x}) ≈ \displaystyle{\sum_{t=1}^{T}}m_t \log p_θ (x_t | {\bf \hat x}) = \displaystyle{\sum_{t=1}^{T}}m_t \log \frac{exp(H_θ({\bf \hat x})_t^{\top} e(x_t))}{ \sum _{x'} exp(H_θ({\bf \hat x})_t^{\top} e(x'))} $
      • $ {\bf x} = [x_1, ..., x_T]$: text sequence
      • $ {\bf \hat x} $: corrupted version where some tokens from $ {\bf x} $ are randomly replaced with [MASK]
      • $ {\bf \bar x} $: masked tokens
      • $ m_t=1 $ if $x_t$ is masked
      • $ H_θ $: Transformer that maps text sequence to hidden vectors
      • $ e(x) $: embedding of x
      • exp is used for softmax
      • This is an approximate factorization.
    • The training objective is to recover $ {\bf \bar x} $ from $ {\bf \hat x} $.

Permutation Language Modeling

Independence Assumption

  • In BERT, all masked tokens are predicted independently.
  • For example, given [New, York, is, a, city] and [New, York] as prediction targets, the objectives for BERT and XLNet are:
    • $ {\cal J}_{BERT} = \log p(\text{New} | \text{is a city}) + \log p(\text{York} | \text{is a city})$
    • $ {\cal J}_{XLNet} = \log p(\text{New} | \text{is a city}) + \log p(\text{York} | \textcolor{red}{\text{New}} \text{, is a city})$
    • In BERT, the prediction for ‘York’ is independent of ‘New’.
    • BERT cannot model the dependency between (‘New’, ‘York’), but XLNet considers these dependencies during training.
    • Using AutoRegressive, you’d have to predict ‘a’ after seeing [New, York, is]. XLNet overcomes such limitations through permutation.

Permutation Language Modeling Objective

  • For a sequence x of length T, there are T! possible orders.
  • By sharing parameters across all factorization orders, the model can learn information in both directions at all positions.
  • Without the independence assumption; and since [MASK] is not used, there is no pretrain-finetune discrepancy.
  • Only the factorization order is permuted; the input sequence order remains unchanged, so positional encoding is based on the original sequence order.
  • The objective:
    • $ \underset{θ}{\max} \Bbb E_{{\bf z} \sim \cal Z_t} [\displaystyle{\sum_{t=1}^{T}} \log p_θ (x_{z_t} | {\bf x_{z \rm < t}})] $
    • $ \cal Z_t $: all possible permutations.
    • $ z_t $: the t-th value in the permutation.
    • After sampling a factorization order ${\bf z}$, likelihood $ \log p_θ $ is computed according to that order.
  • Example: New York is a city
    • Permutation 1: [New, York, is, a, city]. After seeing New → York → is, predict a.
    • Permutation 2: [city, York, New, is, a]. After seeing city → York → New → is, predict a.
    • The model can see city (which comes after a in the sequence) and use it to predict a.
    • By using permutations, the model learns bidirectional context.


Source: Yang, Zhilin, et al. “Xlnet: Generalized autoregressive pretraining for language understanding.” Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019), Figure 4.

  • After calculating $ \log p_θ $ with the given factorization order sampled by permutation, the expectation is computed.
  • ‘mem’ is a feature of Transformer-XL, which enables learning of long sequences; gradients are not applied to it.

Two-Stream Self-Attention

Re-parameterization Considering Target Position

  • In a vanilla transformer, if two permutations $ {\bf z}^{(1)} $ and $ {\bf z}^{(2)} $ are identical up to position t, but differ at t, the following problem arises:
    • $ {\bf z}^{(1)}_t $ and $ {\bf z}^{(2)}_t $ have different target positions and ground truths, but yield the same model prediction.
  • To address this, instead of only using $ {\bf x_{z \rm < t}} $, XLNet introduces a new representation $ g_θ({\bf x_{z \rm < t}}, z_t) $ that also receives the target position $ z_t $ as input:
    • $ p_θ (X_{x_{z_t}}=x | x_{z < t}) = \frac{exp(e(x)^{\top} g_θ({\bf x_{z \rm < t}}, z_t))}{ \sum _{x'} exp(e(x')^{\top} g_θ({\bf x _{z \rm < t}}, z_t) )}$

Using Two Hidden Representations

  • For $ g_θ({\bf x_{z < t}}, z_t) $:
    • If the content $ x_{z_t} $ is used, no learning will occur, so only the position $ z_t $ is used, not the content $ x_{z_t} $.
    • To predict $ x_{z_j} $ for $j>t$, the content $ x_{z_t}$ is not used, but the contextual information must be available.
  • Hidden representations:
    • Content representation $ h_θ({\bf x_{z \leq t}}) $: Acts similarly to the hidden state in a standard Transformer, encoding both the context and $ x_{z_t} $.
    • Query representation $ g_θ({\bf x_{z < t}}, z_t) $: Encodes the contextual information prior to $ t $ and the position $ z_t $.
    • $ h_i^{(0)}=e(x_i) $. For the content stream, the first layer is the word embedding.
    • $ g_i^{(0)}=w $. For the query stream, the first layer is initialized as a learnable vector.
  • Update:
    • Query stream: $ g_{z_t}^{(m)} \leftarrow \text{Attention}(Q=g_{z_t}^{(m-1)}, KV=h_{{\bf z} < t}^{(m-1)}; θ)$
    • Content stream: $ h_{z_t}^{(m)} \leftarrow \text{Attention}(Q=h_{z_t}^{(m-1)}, KV=h_{{\bf z} \leq t}^{(m-1)}; θ)$
    • In the query stream, $z_t$ is used, but $ x_{z_t} $ is not. On the other hand, in the content stream, both $z_t$ and $ x_{z_t} $ are used.


Source: Yang, Zhilin, et al. “Xlnet: Generalized autoregressive pretraining for language understanding.” Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019), Figure 1.

  • When the factorization order is 3 → 2 → 4 → 1:
  • In the next layer, $h_3^{(1)}$ attends to all previous layer states up to and including itself; i.e., it attends to $h_1^{(0)}, h_2^{(0)}, h_3^{(0)}$.
  • In the next layer, $g_3^{(1)}$ only attends to prior positions in the previous layer; i.e., $h_1^{(0)}, h_2^{(0)}$.
  • In the final layer, predictions are made from g.
  • With this attention mechanism, the prediction layer cannot access the current word embedding directly, which encourages better learning.


Source: Yang, Zhilin, et al. “Xlnet: Generalized autoregressive pretraining for language understanding.” Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019), Figure 5.

Source: Yang, Zhilin, et al. “Xlnet: Generalized autoregressive pretraining for language understanding.” Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019), Figure 6.

item.title: A Review of GPT1, GPT2, GPT3
item.description:
In this post, we will review the model architecture, training methods, and major improvements of GPT1, GPT2, and GPT3 introduced by OpenAI.

  • GPT1: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training (https://www.mikecaptain.com/resources/pdf/GPT-1.pdf)
  • GPT2: Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners (https://storage.prod.researchhub.com/uploads/papers/2020/06/01/language-models.pdf)
  • GPT3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2005.14165)

Table of Contents

  1. GPT1
  2. GPT2
  3. GPT3

GPT1

GPT1 is the first Generative Pre-trained Transformer model introduced by OpenAI. It is a language model that combines large-scale unsupervised pre-training and small-scale supervised fine-tuning.

Pre-Training

  • Trained in an unsupervised manner.
  • Acts as a good initialization point for subsequent supervised fine-tuning.
  • Maximizes the following likelihood:
    • $ L_1(\mathcal{U}) = \displaystyle{\sum_{i}} \log P(u_i|u_{i-k}, ..., u_{i-1} ; \Theta)$
      • $ \mathcal{U}= u_1, ..., u_n $: tokens of the unsupervised corpus
      • k: context window size (512)
      • $\Theta$: neural network parameters
  • Uses a multi-layer Transformer decoder as the language model:
    • $ h_0 = U W_e + W_p \\\ h_l = \mathrm{transformer \\_ block}(h_{l-1}) \forall i \in [1, n] \\\ P(u) = \mathrm{softmax}(h_n W_e^T)$
      • $ U=(u_{-k}, ..., u_{-1}) $
      • $n$: number of layers
      • $W_e$ : token embedding matrix
      • $W_p$ : position embedding matrix
  • Dataset: BooksCorpus
    • Used the ftfy library to fix corrupted unicode text and performed other cleaning steps.
    • Standardized punctuation and whitespace.

Fine-Tuning

  • The target model for downstream tasks is trained as follows:
    • $ P(y|x^1, ..., x^m) = \mathrm{softmax}(h_l^m W_y) $
      • $y$ : label
      • $ x^1, ..., x^m $ : input tokens
      • $ h^m_l $: last transformer block’s activation
      • $W_y$ : parameters of an additional linear layer
  • Supervised objective: Model is trained to maximize the following objective.
    • $ L_2(\mathcal{C}) = \displaystyle{\sum_{(x, y)}} \log P(y|x^1, ..., x^m) $
      • $ \mathcal{C} $: labeled dataset
  • The overall objective uses the following auxiliary objective:
    • $ L_3(\mathcal{C}) = L_2(\mathcal{C}) + \lambda * L_1(\mathcal{C})$

Task-specific input transformations

Source: Radford, Alec, et al. “Improving language understanding by generative pre-training.” (2018), Figure 1.

  • Traversal-style:
    • When inputs consist of multiple independent items, such as (premise, hypothesis) or (context, question, answer), using them as-is may not match the form used during pre-training, making them harder for the model to interpret.
    • For each task, input items are concatenated into a single ordered sequence for training.
  • Most tasks were trained for 3 epochs.

Model Architecture

  • Decoder-only transformer
  • Masked self-attention: 768 dimension, 12 attention heads
  • Position-wise feed-forward network: 3072 dimension
  • Adam optimization
  • Batch size: 64
  • Trained on sequences of 512 continuous tokens
  • Layer norm, simple weight initialization N(0, 0.02)
  • Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)
  • Dropout: 0.01
  • Modified L2 regularization
  • GELU activation

GPT2

GPT2 introduced a deeper and larger model, trained on a large-scale web dataset (WebText), and demonstrated high performance using only the pre-trained model without separate fine-tuning. It gained attention for its strong zero-shot performance.

Training

  • Dataset: WebText, a dataset collected from the web.

    • Collected links from Reddit that received at least 3 karma.
    • 8 million documents, total of 40GB.
    • Performed de-duplication.
    • Wikipedia data was excluded because it is a frequent source in WebText.
    • Used a modified Byte Pair Encoding (BPE).

  • Model

    • Layer normalization changed position to the input and an additional layer norm was applied after the last self-attention block.
    • For a residual layer with N blocks, initialization was scaled by $ 1/\sqrt{N} $.
    • Uses modified byte-level BPE.
    • Vocabulary expanded to 50,257.
    • Context window size increased from 512 to 1,024.
    • Batch size increased to 512, compared to GPT1.
    • Model parameter sizes are as follows:

      Source: Radford, Alec, et al. “Language models are unsupervised multitask learners.” OpenAI blog 1.8 (2019): 9, Table 2.

Results

  • Zero-shot performance achieved state-of-the-art on 7 out of 8 benchmarks. Source: Radford, Alec, et al. “Language models are unsupervised multitask learners.” OpenAI blog 1.8 (2019): 9, Table 3.

GPT3

GPT3 massively scaled the size of the transformer model to hundreds of billions of parameters. Leveraging a huge amount of data and parameters, it demonstrated few-shot and zero-shot learning capabilities, performing various tasks using only prompts without any fine-tuning.

Zero-shot, One-shot, Few-shot

  • Can perform various tasks with just prompts and no additional fine-tuning. Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Figure 2.1.

Training

  • Dataset: CommonCrawl

    • Applied filtering based on similarity to high-quality corpora.
    • Reduced from 45TB (before filtering) to 570GB (after filtering).
    • Fuzzy deduplication at the document level.
    • Added well-known high-quality datasets (expanded WebText, Books1, Books2, English Wikipedia) for greater diversity. These datasets were upsampled for training. Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Table 2.2.

  • Model

    • Model sizes are as follows:
      Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Table 2.1.
    • Similar architecture to GPT2; Sparse Transformer was used.
    • Adam optimization.
    • Cosine decay.
    • Linear learning rate warmup.
    • Weight decay.
    • Batch size increased from 32k tokens up to 4-12 billion tokens.
    • Data is sampled without replacement, and “end of text” token is used when concatenating different documents.
    • Context window $n_{ctx}$=2048, increased compared to GPT2.

Evaluation

  • Sampled k examples from the training data, concatenated with \n, where k: 10~100.
  • Selected the best-performing k on the dev set and applied it to the test set for final evaluation. Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Figure 3.4. Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Figure 3.5. Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Figure 3.9. Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Figure 1.2. Source: Brown, Tom, et al. “Language models are few-shot learners.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.14165 (2020): 4, Figure 1.3.

item.title: BERT Review
item.description:
In this post, we’ll take a look at “BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding”, published by Google in 2018.
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1810.04805.pdf

Table of Contents

  1. Model Architecture
  2. Input
  3. Pre-training
  4. Fine-tuning
  5. Network Structure
  6. Training
  7. Testing

Model Architecture


Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Figure 3.

  • GPT: Uses a left-to-right Transformer.

  • ELMo: Trains left-to-right and right-to-left LSTMs separately and then concatenates them. This is a feature-based approach.

  • BERT

    • Uses a bi-directional Transformer.
    • Overcomes the challenge of a bi-directional model referencing its own token (which makes naive predictions impossible) by introducing two pre-training tasks.
    • BERT base has 110M parameters, and BERT large has 340M parameters.

  • Differences between GPT and BERT:

    • GPT was trained only on BooksCorpus (800M words), while BERT used both BooksCorpus and Wikipedia (2,500M words).
    • GPT used [SEP] and [CLS] tokens only for fine-tuning, but BERT also used these in pre-training, along with sentence A/B embeddings.
    • GPT was trained for 1M steps with a batch size of 32,000 words; BERT was trained for the same number of steps, but with a batch size of 128,000 words.
    • GPT used the same learning rate for all fine-tuning tasks, while BERT used task-specific learning rates.

Input


Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Figure 2.

  • Token embeddings:
    • Uses WordPiece embeddings (with a 30,000 token vocabulary).
    • The vocab_size is 30,522 (it looks like 522 tokens are reserved/unused).
    • [CLS]: Every sequence starts with a [CLS] token, which is used for classification tasks.
    • [SEP]: Used to separate sentences within a sequence.
  • Segment Embeddings: Distinguishes between the first sentence and the second sentence.
  • Position Embeddings: Indicates the positional distance of each token.

Pre-training

  • Pre-training uses unlabeled data with two unsupervised tasks.

    Task 1: Masked LM (MLM)

    • Randomly masks 15% of the input tokens, then predicts those masked tokens using a softmax over the vocabulary based on the final hidden vector.
    • Since there are no [MASK] tokens during fine-tuning, of the chosen 15%, 80% are replaced by [MASK], 10% by a random token, and the remaining 10% are left unchanged for prediction.
    • The figure below shows an example where the sequence “my dog is cutep [SEP] he likes play ###ing[SEP]” is masked and predicted:
      Source: https://wikidocs.net/115055
      • ‘dog’ is replaced with [MASK].
      • ‘he’ is replaced with ‘king’.
      • ‘play’ is left unchanged.
      • The last hidden vectors for ‘dog’, ‘he’, and ‘play’ go through a fully connected layer and softmax to predict probabilities over the vocabulary.

    Task 2: Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)

    • When choosing sentences A and B, 50% of the data uses the actual next sentence, while the other 50% uses a random sentence from the corpus as B.

    • Sample:

      Input = [CLS] the man went to [MASK] store [SEP]
      he bought a gallon [MASK] milk [SEP]
      Label = IsNext

      Input = [CLS] the man [MASK] to the store [SEP]
      penguin [MASK] are flight ##less birds [SEP]
      Label = NotNext

    • The final hidden vector of the [CLS] token is used for IsNext/NotNext classification.
      Source: https://wikidocs.net/115055

Fine-tuning

  • After initializing with pre-trained parameters, BERT is fine-tuned with labeled data for downstream tasks.

  • Only a small number of parameters are newly learned, since just a single output layer is added on top.

    Sentence Pair Classification Tasks

    • Datasets
      • MNLI: Classifies the relationship of the second sentence to the first as entailment, contradiction, or neutral.
      • QQP: Checks if two questions are semantically equivalent.
      • QNLI: Checks whether there is a correct answer to the question.
      • STS-B: Scores sentence similarity on a scale from 1 to 5.
      • MRPC: Checks if two sentences are semantically equivalent.
      • RTE: Determines if the second sentence is true, false, or unknown, given the first.
      • SWAG: Given a sentence, chooses which of four options is the most likely next sentence.
    • C is the final hidden vector of the [CLS] token, $C\in\mathbb{R}^H$. H is the hidden size (768 for BERT_BASE, 1024 for BERT_LARGE).
    • The only new parameters to be trained are the classification layer weights $W\in\mathbb R^{K \times H}$, where K is the number of classes.
      Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Figure 4, (a).
    • Results
      Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Table 1.

      Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Table 4.

    Single Sentence Classification Tasks

    • Datasets
      • SST-2: Sentiment classification.
      • CoLA: Determines if the sentence is linguistically “acceptable”.
    • Just like in sentence pair tasks, the classification layer weights $W\in\mathbb R^{K \times H}$ are trained during fine-tuning.

      Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Figure 4, (b).

    Question Answering Tasks

    • Datasets
      • SQuAD v1.1: Answers are found in the passage for each question.
      • SQuAD v2.0
    • The question is represented as an A embedding, and the passage as a B embedding.
    • During fine-tuning, the start vector $S \in \mathbb{R}^H$ and end vector $E \in \mathbb{R}^H$ are learned.
    • To determine start and end positions, the dot product and softmax are calculated between each token’s final hidden vector T and S or E, respectively.

      Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Figure 4, (c).

    Single Sentence Tagging Tasks

    • Dataset
      • CoNLL-2003 NER

        Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Figure 4, (d).
    • Results

      Source: Devlin, Jacob, et al. “Bert: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.04805 (2018), Table 7.

item.title: YOLOv3 Review
item.description:
In this post, we will look at the paper “YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement”, published in 2018.
YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement

Table of Contents

  1. Prediction at Multiple Scales
  2. Results

Prediction at Multiple Scales


Source: https://wikidocs.net/174008

  • YOLOv3 predicts bounding boxes at three different scales, similar to a Feature Pyramid Network.
  • Several convolutional layers are attached to the base feature extractor, with the final layer predicting bounding boxes, objectness, and class probabilities.
  • In this paper, 3 anchor boxes are used per grid cell. Therefore, if N is the number of grids in the feature map, each feature map predicts N×N×\[3 * (4+1+80)\] values.
  • Since 3 types of anchors are used at 3 different scales, there are a total of 9 anchors. Anchors are obtained using k-means clustering with k=9.
  • For the COCO dataset, the following anchor boxes are used: (10×13), (16×30), (33×23), (30×61), (62×45), (59×119), (116×90), (159×198), (373×326).

Results


Source: Redmon, Joseph, and Ali Farhadi. “Yolov3: An incremental improvement.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1804.02767 (2018), Table 3.

item.title: YOLOv2 Review
item.description:
In this post, we’ll take a look at the paper “YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger” presented at CVPR 2017.
YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger

Table of Contents

  1. Motivation
  2. Batch Normalization
  3. Classifier Training on High Resolution
  4. Use of Anchor Boxes
  5. Dimension Clusters
  6. Predicted Values
  7. Fine-Grained Features
  8. Network
  9. Results

Motivation

YOLOv1, compared to the then state-of-the-art Fast-RCNN, had considerable localization errors and relatively low recall, frequently missing objects. In YOLOv2, several methods were designed to solve these problems of localization error and low recall, while maintaining a fast inference speed and without increasing network size.

Batch Normalization

  • See Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift
  • Batch Normalization was proposed to address covariate shift by normalizing the input to each layer within a mini-batch to the mini-batch’s mean and variance.
  • Since its introduction in 2015 (PMLR), it has been widely used.
  • In YOLOv2, adding Batch Normalization increased mAP by 2%.
  • Batch normalization also acted as a regularizer, so dropout could be removed without causing overfitting.

Classifier Training on High Resolution

  • Object detection models are usually pre-trained as classifiers on ImageNet, then fine-tuned on detection tasks using transfer learning.
  • YOLOv1 also followed this, using 224×224 images for classification and 448×448 images for detection. This means that during detection training, the network needs to learn not only detection but also to adapt to the changed input resolution.
  • To overcome this, YOLOv2 pre-trained the classification model on 448×448 ImageNet images for 10 epochs before detection training. This simple step improved mAP by 4%.

Use of Anchor Boxes

  • YOLOv1 directly predicted bounding box coordinates by attaching a Fully Connected layer to the feature extractor.
  • YOLOv2 removes the Fully Connected layer and, instead, uses anchor boxes to learn offsets. For each anchor, the model predicts class and objectness.
  • As a result, mAP slightly dropped from 69.5 to 69.2, but recall significantly increased from 81% to 88%.

Dimension Clusters

  • Instead of manually selecting anchor boxes, YOLOv2 uses k-means clustering on training set bounding boxes to derive anchor boxes. Instead of Euclidean distance, it uses a custom metric to avoid a bias for larger boxes:

    • $ d(box, centroid) = 1 - IOU(box, centroid) $

  • Below is a graph of mean IOU (between dataset bounding boxes and their nearest centroid) and the anchor boxes for k=5:

    Source: Redmon, Joseph, and Ali Farhadi. “YOLO9000: better, faster, stronger.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2017, Figure 2

  • Increasing k improves performance but increases complexity; the paper uses k=5 as a tradeoff.

  • The white boxes in the above figure show VOC 2007 anchors and the blue boxes COCO anchors at k=5. This shows anchor shapes differ by dataset.


    Source: Redmon, Joseph, and Ali Farhadi. “YOLO9000: better, faster, stronger.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2017, Table 1

  • The table above shows average IOU between ground truth and the closest anchor for various anchor generation methods on VOC 2007.

  • “Cluster SSE” refers to k-means using sum of squared errors (Euclidean distance); “Cluster IOU” refers to k-means using IOU-based distance; “Anchor Boxes” refers to the nine manually chosen anchors in 15.

  • IOU-based k-means yields higher average IOU than both SSE-based k-means and the manually-chosen anchor boxes.

Predicted Values

Before getting into the outputs YOLOv2 predicts, let’s define the notation:

  • $ (c_x, c_y) $: coordinates of the top-left corner of the grid cell
  • $ (b_x, b_y) $: center coordinates of the bounding box
  • $ (b_w, b_h) $: width and height of the bounding box
  • $ (p_w, p_h) $: prior width and height of the anchor box
  • $ (t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h, t_o) $: YOLOv2 network outputs (denoted with t for “target”)

The predictions:

  • $ b_x = \sigma(t_x) + c_x $
  • $ b_y = \sigma(t_y) + c_y $
  • $ b_w = p_w e^{t_w}$
  • $ b_h = p_h e^{t_h}$
  • $ Pr(object) \cdot IOU(b, object) = \sigma(t_o)$

    Source: Redmon, Joseph, and Ali Farhadi. “YOLO9000: better, faster, stronger.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2017, Figure 3
  • For bounding box centers, a logistic activation ($\sigma$) is used, so the predictions are between 0 and 1 and specify a relative position in the cell. For width and height, the output is exponentiated and then multiplied by the prior (anchor) size.

Fine-Grained Features

A passthrough layer was added to allow detection to use features at different resolutions.

  • The 13×13 feature map for detection is connected to the previous 26×26 feature map via a passthrough layer.
  • The 26×26×512 feature map is reshaped to 13×13×2048 and concatenated with the 13×13 feature map.

Network

  • VGG-16 was used as the base feature extractor. It requires 30.69 billion floating point operations per image.
  • The paper proposes Darknet-19, with 19 convolutional layers, requiring only 5.58 billion operations per image.

Results


Source: Redmon, Joseph, and Ali Farhadi. “YOLO9000: better, faster, stronger.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2017, Table 2

  • This table shows the mAP for different model types on VOC 2007.
  • The mAP is significantly improved compared to YOLOv1.


Source: Redmon, Joseph, and Ali Farhadi. “YOLO9000: better, faster, stronger.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2017, Table 3


Source: [Redmon, Joseph, and Ali Farhadi. “YOLO9000: better, faster, stronger.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2017, Figure 4](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content_cvpr_2017/papers/Redmon_YOLO9000_Better_F

item.title: YOLOv1 Review
item.description:
In this post, we’ll take a look at the paper “You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection” presented at CVPR 2016.
https://www.cv-foundation.org/openaccess/content_cvpr_2016/papers/Redmon_You_Only_Look_CVPR_2016_paper.pdf

Table of Contents

  1. Overview
  2. Bounding Box
  3. Class Probability
  4. Output
  5. Network Architecture
  6. Loss
  7. Testing

Overview

YOLO uses a grid to detect objects in an image. In short:

  • The input image is divided into S×S grid cells.
  • Each grid cell predicts the coordinates (x, y, w, h) for B bounding boxes and a confidence score (c) for each bounding box.
  • Each grid cell also predicts C class probabilities.
  • The final score and class are determined by multiplying the confidence score and class probability. Bounding boxes with scores above a threshold become the final detection results.

  • The above image shows an example with S=7 and B=2 for a single grid cell.
  • Each grid cell predicts two sets of bounding boxes (x, y, w, h, c).
  • For C=20, each grid cell predicts class probabilities for 20 classes.

This is a brief overview of how YOLO finds objects. Below, we’ll look at how each prediction value is calculated and what they mean.

Bounding Box

A bounding box is composed of (x, y, w, h, c).

  • (x, y): Coordinates for the center of the bounding box.
    • Each grid cell has B bounding boxes, meaning the center of each of these B bounding boxes is inside that grid cell.
    • The corners of the box can be outside the grid cell.
    • (x, y) specifies the relative location within the grid cell — values range from 0 to 1.
  • (w, h): Width and height of the bounding box.
    • These are relative to the width and height of the entire image, and are values from 0 to 1.
  • c: Represents how confident the model is that the box contains an object and how accurately the predicted box fits the object.
    • $ Pr(Object) * IOU^{truth}_{pred} $
    • The IOU (Intersection Over Union) between the predicted box and the ground truth (GT) box is calculated to measure localization accuracy.

Class Probability

  • C: The class probabilities for each grid cell when it contains an object.
    • $ Pr(Class_i|Object) $
    • Each grid cell predicts only one set of class probabilities, regardless of the number of bounding boxes.

Output

  • If the image is divided into S×S grid cells, and each grid cell predicts B bounding boxes and C class probabilities, the final output tensor shape is S×S×(B*5+C).
  • For example, in PASCAL VOC, S=7, B=2, C=20, so there are 49 grid cells, each predicting 30 values. Here is how one such prediction might look:
    • \[x, y, w, h, c, probability for background, probability for airplane, ..., probability for monitor\]
    • \[0.4, 0.3, 0.8, 0.7, 0.9, 0.003, 0.8, ... , 0.012\]

Network Architecture

  • YOLO uses an architecture similar to GoogLeNet.
    Source: Redmon, Joseph, et al. “You only look once: Unified, real-time object detection.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2016, Figure 3.

Loss

  • Uses sum-squared error.

  • $ \lambda_{coor} $, $ \lambda_{noobj} $ are used.

    • Most images have more grid cells without objects than with objects. The gradient for the confidence score in cells without objects becomes much larger than for cells with objects when the confidence is near 0, which can cause the model to diverge early in training. To address this, they used $ \lambda_{coor}=5 $ to increase the bounding box coordinate loss and $ \lambda_{noobj}=0.5 $ to decrease the confidence loss for boxes without objects.

  • Predicts $ \sqrt w $, $ \sqrt h $

    • Small errors for large boxes are less significant, but for small boxes, the impact is much larger. To address this, the width and height values are square-rooted.

  • Only one bounding box predictor (the one with the highest IOU for each object) is assigned to each object.

  • Loss function
    $ \lambda_{coor} \displaystyle{\sum_{i=0}^{s^2}} \displaystyle{\sum_{j=0}^{B}} \mathbb 1_{ij}^{obj} [(x_i-\hat x_i)^2 + (y_i-\hat y_i)^2] $
    $ + \lambda_{coor} \displaystyle{\sum_{i=0}^{s^2}} \displaystyle{\sum_{j=0}^{B}} \mathbb 1_{ij}^{obj} [(\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat w_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat h_i})^2] $
    $ + \displaystyle{\sum_{i=0}^{s^2}} \displaystyle{\sum_{j=0}^{B}} \mathbb 1_{ij}^{obj} (C_i-\hat C_i)^2 $ $ + \lambda_{noobj} \displaystyle{\sum_{i=0}^{s^2}} \displaystyle{\sum_{j=0}^{B}} \mathbb 1_{ij}^{noobj} (C_i-\hat C_i)^2 $
    $ + \displaystyle{\sum_{i=0}^{s^2}} \mathbb 1_{i}^{obj} \displaystyle{\sum_{c∈classwa}} (p_i(c)-\hat p_i(c))^2 $

    • 1obji: Indicates whether there is an object in cell i.
    • 1objij: Indicates whether the j-th bounding box in cell i is responsible for predicting the ground truth in cell i, i.e., whether that bounding box has the highest IOU with the GT.

Testing

  • $ Pr(Class_i|Object) * Pr(Object) * {IOU}^{truth}_{pred} = Pr({Class}_i) * {IOU}^{truth}\_{pred} $
  • At test time, the final score for each box is computed by multiplying the class probability and the confidence score.
  • The final score reflects both the probability of the class being present in the box, and how well the predicted box fits the object.
  • In the figure below, the thickness of the bounding box lines indicates the confidence. Multiplying this confidence score and the class probability, results above the threshold become the final outcomes.
    Source: Redmon, Joseph, et al. “You only look once: Unified, real-time object detection.” Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2016, Figure 2.

item.title: YOLOv5 Loss
item.description:
In this post, we will look at the Loss function of YOLOv5.

Table of Contents

  1. Information Theory
  2. Binary Cross Entropy
  3. IoU Loss
  4. YOLOv5 Loss

Information Theory

  • Information

    • If the probability of an event A occurring is P(A), the amount of information for this event is $I=-\log P(A)$.
    • Example: Information when a coin lands on heads
      • Probability: $ \displaystyle \frac{1}{2}$
      • $I=-\log \displaystyle \frac{1}{2}$

  • Entropy

    • The expected value of the information.
    • $ H(X)=- \displaystyle \sum_{x}P(X) \log P(X)$
    • Example: Entropy of a coin flip
      • $Entropy= \displaystyle{ -(\frac{1}{2} \times \log \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \times \log \frac{1}{2})=-\log \frac{1}{2}}$

  • Kullback–Leibler divergence (KL Divergence)

    • Measures the difference between two probability distributions.
    • $ D_{KL}(P \Vert Q)= \displaystyle \sum_{x}P(X) \log \frac{P(X)}{Q(X)} $
                                $ = -\displaystyle \sum_{x}P(X) \log Q(X) +\sum_{x} P(X) \log P(X)$
                               
      • The first term is the entropy calculated using distribution Q for data from distribution P.
      • The second term is the entropy of the distribution P.
      • In other words, KL divergence tells you how much more information you have when using Q to encode P compared to using P itself.

  • Cross Entropy

    • The first term of the KL divergence above, i.e. the expected value of information when using distribution Q for data from distribution P, is called the Cross Entropy.
    • $ H(P, Q)= -\displaystyle \sum_{x}P(X)\log Q(X) $
    • KL divergence can be rearranged as below:
      • $ D_{KL}(P \Vert Q)= H(P, Q) - H(P) $
      • Here, P is the true distribution, Q is the distribution produced by an approximate model, and H(P) is constant. Therefore, cross-entropy H(P, Q) is often used as a model’s loss when minimizing KL divergence.

Binary Cross Entropy

  • Cross Entropy Loss
    • In classification, the data label y is given as a one-hot vector, where y is probability distribution P in the formula for cross entropy above.
    • The model output $\hat y$ is typically the softmax output probabilities for each class, i.e., distribution Q.
    • When N is the number of data samples, y the labels, and C the number of classes, the Cross Entropy Loss L is:
      • $ L= - \displaystyle \sum_{n}^{N} \displaystyle \sum_{c}^{C}y_{n, c} \log \hat{y}_{n, c}$
    • For example, in a classification problem with ‘dog’, ‘cat’, and ‘frog’:
      • $y=\begin{bmatrix} 0 \cr 1 \cr 0 \end{bmatrix} $ (True label)
      • $\hat y=\begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \cr 0.7 \cr 0.2 \end{bmatrix} $ (Prediction)
      • The cross-entropy loss $l$ for this image is:
        • $ l= -\displaystyle \sum_{c}^{C}y_c\log \hat{y}_c$
          $=0⋅\log 0.1 + 1⋅\log 0.7 + 0⋅\log 0.2$
          $=\log 0.7$
  • Binary Cross Entropy
    • A special case of cross entropy loss when the number of classes is two.
    • The model’s output $\hat y$ is usually the probability from a sigmoid function.
    • $ L= - \displaystyle \sum_{n}^{N} [y_n\log \hat{y}_n + (1-y_n)\log (1-\hat{y}_n)]$
  • BCEWithLogitsLoss
    • Torch provides the BCEWithLogitsLoss class, which combines the Sigmoid layer and the Binary Cross Entropy.
    • It is more numerically stable than using Sigmoid and BCELoss separately, due to the log-sum-exp trick.
    • If x is the input to Sigmoid, y the label, N the number of samples, and w the weight, the unreduced loss L is:
      • $ L=\begin{Bmatrix} l_{1}, \cr ..., \cr l_{N} \end{Bmatrix}$ where $l_n= - w_n [(y_n\log \sigma (x_n)) + (1-y_n)\log (1-\sigma(x_n))]$
    • The reduced loss L is:
      • $ L=\begin{cases} mean(L), \text{if reduction='mean'} \cr sum(L), \text{if reduction= 'sum'} \end{cases} $
    • With c as the class, you can adjust precision and recall by using a positive weight $p_c$. The formula becomes:
      • $ L_c=\begin{Bmatrix} l_{1,c}, \cr ..., \cr l_{N, c} \end{Bmatrix}$ where $l_{n, c}= - w_{n,c} [(p_c ​⋅ y_{n, c}\log \sigma (x_n)) + (1-y_{n, c})\log (1-\sigma(x_{n, c}))]$
      • $p_c>1$ increases recall, $p_c<1$ increases precision.
      • If there are 100 positive and 300 negative examples for a class, set $p_c=\frac{300}{100}=3$.
    • torch.nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss(weight=None, size_average=None, reduce=None, reduction=‘mean’, pos_weight=None)
      • size_average, reduce are deprecated parameters. 
        1
2
3
4
5
6

        target = torch.ones([10, 64], dtype=torch.float32) # 64 classes, batch size = 10
output = torch.full([10, 64], 1.5) # A prediction (logit)
pos_weight = torch.ones([64]) # All weights are equal to 1
criterion = torch.nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss(pos_weight=pos_weight)
criterion(output, target) # -log(sigmoid(1.5))
# tensor(0.20...)

IoU Loss

  • Intersection over Union (IoU)

    • Scale invariant.
    • When you have convex A and B, IoU is calculated as follows:
      • $ IoU= \displaystyle \frac{|A∩B|}{|A∪B|} $
    • $L_{IoU}=1-IoU$
    • When IoU is 0, i.e., when the target box and prediction box do not overlap in object detection, IoU does not indicate how large the error is. As a result, the Gradient Vanishing problem occurs.

  • Generalized-IoU (GIoU)

    • Generalized Intersection Over Union: A Metric and a Loss for Bounding Box Regression
    • If C is the smallest convex that contains A and B, GIoU is calculated as:
      • $ GIoU=IoU- \displaystyle \frac{|C \backslash (A∪B)|}{|C|} $
      • $ C \backslash (A∪B)$ means set difference.
    • $L_{GIoU}=1-GIoU$. The range of loss value is 0~2.
    • If the target box contains the prediction box, the penalty term is zero and $L_{GIoU}$ becomes the same as $L_{IoU}$.
      Source: Zheng, Zhaohui, et al. “Distance-IoU loss: Faster and better learning for bounding box regression.” Proceedings of the AAAI conference on artificial intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 07. 2020, Figure 2
    • Has slow convergence and comparatively low box accuracy.

  • Distance-IoU (DIoU) & Complete IoU (CIoU)

    • Distance-IoU Loss: Faster and Better Learning for Bounding Box Regression
    • In general, IoU-based loss can be defined as $L_{IoU}=1-IoU + \mathcal{R}(B, B^{gt})$.
    • DIoU
      • $ \mathcal{R}_{DIoU} = \displaystyle \frac{ρ^2(b, b^{gt})}{c^2}$. ρ is the Euclidean distance between the centers of the two boxes, and c is the diagonal length of the smallest box enclosing both boxes.

        Source: Zheng, Zhaohui, et al. “Distance-IoU loss: Faster and better learning for bounding box regression.” Proceedings of the AAAI conference on artificial intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 07. 2020, Figure 5
      • $L_{DIoU}=1-IoU + \mathcal{R}_{DIoU}$, range is 0~2.
      • Since the distance between boxes is minimized, it converges faster than GIoU loss.
    • CIoU
      • This is an extension of DIoU to additionally consider the aspect ratio.
      • $ \mathcal{R}_{CIoU} = \displaystyle \frac{ρ^2(b, b^{gt})}{c^2} + \alpha v$
        • $\alpha = \displaystyle \frac{v}{(1-IoU) + v}$
          • Gives higher priority to regression in non-overlapping cases.
        • $v = \displaystyle \frac{4}{\pi^2}(arctan\frac{w^{gt}}{h^{gt}} - arctan\frac{w}{h})^2$
          • Measures the similarity of aspect ratios.
          • $ \displaystyle \frac{4}{\pi^2}$ is to make the maximum calculation result 1 since arctan max is $\frac{\pi}{2}$.
      • $L_{CIoU}=1-IoU + \mathcal{R}_{CIoU}$
    • Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) using DIoU
      • In traditional NMS, the IoU metric is used to remove unnecessary detection boxes.
      • Since IoU only considers the overlapping area, incorrect boxes can be suppressed in occlusion situations.
      • DIoU considers both the overlapping area and the center point distance between the two boxes, making it more suitable as a criterion for NMS in case of occlusion.
      • Let $\mathcal{M}$ be the prediction box with the highest score, $B$ other boxes to apply NMS to, $s$ is the classification score, and $\epsilon$ is the NMS threshold. DIoU-NMS is defined as:
        • $ s_i= \begin{cases} \displaystyle s_i, & IoU -\mathcal{R}(\mathcal{M}, B_i) < \epsilon \\\ \displaystyle 0, & IoU -\mathcal{R}(\mathcal{M}, B_i) ≥ \epsilon \end{cases} $
      • Below is an example using a YOLOv3 model trained on PASCAL VOC07+12, detecting an image from MS COCO 2017.
        Source: Zheng, Zhaohui, et al. “Distance-IoU loss: Faster and better learning for bounding box regression.” Proceedings of the AAAI conference on artificial intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 07. 2020, Figure 8
    • Experimental Results
      • Below are results for training YOLOv3 with $ L_{IoU} $, $ L_{GIoU} $, $ L_{DIoU} $, $ L_{CIoU} $. (D) means IoU-NMS is used during NMS.

        Source: Zheng, Zhaohui, et al. “Distance-IoU loss: Faster and better learning for bounding box regression.” Proceedings of the AAAI conference on artificial intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 07. 2020, Table 1
      • Below are results for training SSD with $ L_{IoU} $, $ L_{GIoU} $, $ L_{DIoU} $, $ L_{CIoU} $. (D) means IoU-NMS is used during NMS.

        Source: Zheng, Zhaohui, et al. “Distance-IoU loss: Faster and better learning for bounding box regression.” Proceedings of the AAAI conference on artificial intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 07. 2020, Table 2
      • Below are results for training Faster-RCNN with $ L_{IoU} $, $ L_{GIoU} $, $ L_{DIoU} $, $ L_{CIoU} $. (D) means IoU-NMS is used during NMS.
